coredata.dc:title: A novel nanosensor composed of aptamer bio-dots and gold nanoparticles for determination of thrombin with multiple signals
coredata.dc:creator: Kuang, Lan
Cao, Shu-Ping
Zhang, Li
Li, Qiu-Hong
Liu, Zhi-Chao
Liang, Ru-Ping
Qiu, Jian-Ding
coredata.dc:description: Thrombin is a crucial multifunctional enzyme involved in many physiological and pathological processes. Its detection is of great importance. In this work, a novel bio-dots nanosensor for detection of thrombin with colorimetric, fluorometric and light-scattering signals is developed. This nanosensor is composed of thrombin-binding aptamer bio-dots (TBA-dots) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), where TBA-dots serve as fluorometric reporter and AuNPs function as multiple roles as colorimetric reporter, light scattering reporter and fluorescence quencher. TBA-dots retain inherent structures of aptamer to specifically interact with thrombin and simultaneously induce the aggregation of AuNPs. The mechanism of the sensing system is based on distance-dependent aggregation of AuNPs and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The nanosensor needs no further surface functionalization required for the as-prepared bio-dots and AuNPs, which provides a sensitive method for the selective detection of thrombin with a detection limit as low as 0.59nM. In addition, it provides a brand new perspective for bio-dots and its potential use in bioanalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bio-dots
Aptamer
Thrombin
FRET
Multiple signals

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of thrombin in human serum

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21105044 , 21163014 , and 21265017 ) and the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University ( NCET-11-1002 and NCET-13-0848). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (b) AFM image of the TBA-dots. The inset shows the diameter distribution and the height distribution, respectively. (c) XPS full scan spectrum and (d) the high-resolution O1s XPS spectrum of TBA-dots. (e) FTIR spectrum of TBA-dots. (f) UV–vis absorption spectra of TBA-dots (black dashed line) and AuNPs (red dashed line), FL excitation spectrum and emission spectra of the TBA-dots at different excitation wavelengths. The inset shows the photographs of the TBA-dots under daylight (left) and UV light (right). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Normalized absorption spectra of AuNPs suspension containing 80nM TBA-dots in the presence of different thrombin concentrations. (b) Calibration curve of Δλmax vs. thrombin concentration by route A. (c) LS spectra of AuNPs suspension containing 80nM TBA-dots in the presence of different thrombin concentrations. (d) Calibration curve of LS intensity vs. thrombin concentration by route A. (e) Fluorescence spectra of the TBA-dots containing 2.3nM AuNPs in the presence of different concentrations of thrombin. (f) Calibration curve of fluorescence intensity vs. thrombin concentration by route A. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) UV–vis spectra of AuNPs suspension containing 80nM TBA-dots in the presence of different concentrations of T2 ranging from 0 to 198nM. Inset shows the digital images of change in color of TBA-dots/AuNPs with increased concentrations of T2. (b) Plots of A660/A520 vs. T2 concentration in the TBA-dots/AuNPs suspension. (c) UV–vis spectra of AuNPs suspension containing 80nM TBA-dots and 176nM T2 in the presence of different thrombin concentrations. (d) Calibration curve of A635/A523 vs. thrombin concentration by route B. Inset shows the digital images of change in color of AuNPs with the addition of thrombin. (e) LS spectra of AuNPs suspension containing 80nM TBA-dots and 176nM T2 in the presence of different thrombin concentrations. (f) Calibration curve of LS intensity vs. thrombin concentration by route B. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Zeta potential of AuNPs, TBA, TBA-dots, AuNPs/TBA, and AuNPs/TBA-dots, respectively. The concentrations of AuNPs, TBA, and TBA-dots were 2.3nM, 80nM and 80nM, respectively. (b) CD spectra of TBA-dots in the absence (black solid line) and presence of thrombin (red solid line), T2 (green solid line) and thrombin/T2 (blue solid line) and CD spectra of TBA (orange dashed line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

TEM images of AuNPs in the absence (a), and presence of 80nM TBA-dots (b) and (c), 80nM TBA-dots and 80nM thrombin (d), 80nM TBA-dots and 176nM T2 (e), and 80nM TBA-dots, 176nM T2 and 20nM thrombin (f). The inset in (a) shows a high-resolution TEM image of AuNPs.
Schematic of thrombin detection by using the novel TBA-dots/AuNPs nanosensor with multiple signals readout in two routes.

head.ce:title: A novel nanosensor composed of aptamer bio-dots and gold nanoparticles for determination of thrombin with multiple signals

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Institute for Advanced Study, Nanchang University, Nanchang, Jiangxi 330031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Institute for Advanced Study, Nanchang University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thrombin is a crucial multifunctional enzyme involved in many physiological and pathological processes. Its detection is of great importance. In this work, a novel bio-dots nanosensor for detection of thrombin with colorimetric, fluorometric and light-scattering signals is developed. This nanosensor is composed of thrombin-binding aptamer bio-dots (TBA-dots) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), where TBA-dots serve as fluorometric reporter and AuNPs function as multiple roles as colorimetric reporter, light scattering reporter and fluorescence quencher. TBA-dots retain inherent structures of aptamer to specifically interact with thrombin and simultaneously induce the aggregation of AuNPs. The mechanism of the sensing system is based on distance-dependent aggregation of AuNPs and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). The nanosensor needs no further surface functionalization required for the as-prepared bio-dots and AuNPs, which provides a sensitive method for the selective detection of thrombin with a detection limit as low as 0.59nM. In addition, it provides a brand new perspective for bio-dots and its potential use in bioanalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past two decades, reports focusing on the application of quantum dots (QDs) in biosensing are growing exponentially (Wegner and Hildebrandt, 2015; Zhao et al., 2015) for their unique optical properties, good water solubility and excellent specificity to target biomolecules. QDs have been widely used for determination of DNA (Freeman et al., 2011; Peng et al., 2007), protein and other biomolecules (Wang et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2015), cellular labelling (Wu et al., 2003), bioimaging (Liu et al., 2008; Zhu et al., 2011), and as efficient fluorescence resonance energy transfer donors (Dong et al., 2010; Geißler and Hildebrandt 2016; Shi et al., 2015; Willard et al., 2001). As the most conventional QDs, the fluorescent semiconductor quantum dots containing heavy metals (Pb, Te, Cd, etc.), however, are usually subjected to serious limitations for the noted toxicity and potential environmental hazard (Derfus et al., 2004). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) exhibit many advantages, such as low cytotoxicity, excellent photostability, and green synthetic method, which have proven to be promising substitutes of the heavy-metal-containing semiconductor QDs (Wen et al., 2015). However, the CQDs, as a new type of fluorescent probes, did not show satisfied properties in terms of the sensitivity and selectivity unless they are well functionalized (Li et al., 2013; Qu et al., 2013b). The preparation of functionalized CQDs probe generally suffers from sophisticated processes (Li et al., 2013; Qu et al., 2013b). Hence, the challenges remain to find a new class of fluorescent quantum dots that exhibit greater characteristics in every possible aspect. Recently, a novel fluorescent dots, namely bio-dots was first synthesized which were derived from DNA at relatively low temperatures via DNA self-assembly (Guo et al., 2013). These bio-dots have great potentials of broad biomedical and optical applications due to their low cytotoxicity, high biocompatibility, good solubility, and effective fluorescence (Cheng et al., 2015; Guo et al., 2013; Li et al., 2015; Song et al., 2015). Moreover, the bio-dots have similar fluorescent properties as those of CQDs and hold basic structure of DNA, which would support their applications in building DNA-based nanosensors. Until now, the bio-dots have only been reported for detection of biothiols and glutathione reductase activity (Li et al., 2015), and sensing of metal ions (Fe3+, Ag+, Hg2+) (Cheng et al., 2015; Song et al., 2015). Thus, to construct a novel bio-dots nanosensor with excellent properties for sensing biomolecules has great potential and significance.

Thrombin, one of the immanent proteins in human, is an endoprotease that instinctively functions as a blood clotting factor to transform fibrinogen into fibrin under pathological and physiological conditions (Licari and Kovacic, 2009). Thrombin could serve as a therapeutic agent and a biomarker for diagnosis of many diseases, like pulmonary metastases and diseases associated with coagulation abnormalities (Kitamoto et al., 2008). Numerous approaches, including colorimetric (Wei et al., 2007), fluorescence (Niu et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2008; Wang et al., 2011), electrochemical (Centi et al., 2007; Lin et al., 2010), magnetic (Yigit et al., 2008) and surface enhanced Raman scattering methods (Cho et al., 2008), have been developed to determinate thrombin. Among them, the colorimetric and fluorometric methods draw more attention because of their low cost, high sensitivity, and simple performance. Most of the colorimetric and fluorometric methods were based on the conformational change from a single-stranded thrombin-binding aptamer (TBA) to a quadruplex structure induced by thrombin. AuNPs were used mostly as a colorimetric material and a fluorescence quencher because AuNPs not only exhibit strong distance-dependent optical properties but also have an excellent quenching efficiency in a broad range of wavelengths. For example, a colorimetric sensor for detection thrombin using unmodified AuNPs based on the interaction of the aptamer to thrombin (Wei et al., 2007) and a fluorometric sensor combining AuNPs as fluorescence quencher and aptamer as probe (Wang et al., 2008) have been reported. However, as far as we know, all previously reported aptamer/AuNPs nanosensing systems for detection of thrombin only possessed a single signal, which was not convincing enough because many factors would interfere with the signals readout. Considering this, new nanosensors possessing with multiple signals should be constructed to show better performance.

In this work, a novel class of fluorescent bio-dots named TBA-dots is first synthesized by a green hydrothermal treatment via TBA self-assembly. TBA-dots exhibit comparable fluorescence properties as those of CQDs and retain the main structures of TBA to bind specifically with thrombin. TBA-dots could assemble onto the surface of AuNPs through Au-N bond. Moreover, plentiful TBA-dots would induce the aggregation of AuNPs since that plentiful TBA-dots adsorb onto the surface of AuNPs to form a “shell” which reduces the electrostatic repulsion between adjacent AuNPs. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of TBA-dots would be quenched by AuNPs via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). On the basis of this phenomenon, a novel TBA-dots/AuNPs nanosensor with colorimetric, fluorometric and light-scattering signals for detecting thrombin has been developed ( Scheme 1). In route A, in the presence of thrombin, thrombin acts as bridge that links the TBA-dots/AuNPs together since it includes two binding sites for the aptamers, inducing more AuNPs aggregating along with the decrease in peak intensity of AuNPs at 520nm and red shift of the maximum absorption peak for AuNPs. As a result, the FRET efficiency is decreased because of the reduced spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of TBA-dots and the absorbance spectrum of AuNPs, and then the fluorescene recovery of TBA-dots occurs. Therefore, thrombin can be determinated based on the aggregation degree of AuNPs with colorimetric and light-scattering signals and based on the fluorescene recovery degree of TBA-dots with fluorometric signals. In route B, T2 hybridizes with TBA since that T2 is fully complementary to TBA, resulting in desorption of TBA-dots from the surface of AuNPs and thus AuNPs are fully dispersed in solution. In this case, thrombin specifically interacts with TBA-dots instead of T2, resulting in adsorption of more TBA-dots onto the surface of AuNPs. Simultaneously, thrombin acts as bridges linking TBA-dots/AuNPs together, leading to aggregation of AuNPs. Thus, thrombin can be determinated based on the aggregation degree of AuNPs with colorimetric and light scattering signals. Remarkably, it seems impossible to construct a nanosensor with multiple signals based on TBA only. Our results show that the TBA-dots/AuNPs nanosensor with multiple signals allows the detection of thrombin as low as 0.59nM and performs with high selectivity.

29-mer thrombin-binding aptamer (5′-AGT CCG TGG TAG GGC AGG TTG GGG TGA CT-3′, herein referred to as TBA) and the complementary strand of TBA (5′-AGT CAC CCC AAC CTG CCC TAC CAC GGA CT-3′, herein referred to as T2) were synthesized by Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tetrachloroauric acid trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O) and thrombin (Tb) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Trisodium citrate was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was obtained from Shanghai Bio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other reagents are of analytical grade. The ultrapure water with an electrical resistance larger than 18.2 MΩ was used in all the experiments.

The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence (FL) spectra and light-scattering (LS) spectra were obtained by a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The morphologies of TBA-dots and AuNPs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) using a JEM-2010 (Tokyo, Japan). The atomic force microscope (AFM) images were recorded using a Bruker MultiMode-8 AFM/SPM (Ettlingen, Germany) with the ScanAsyst mode. The dark field light-scattering images were obtained using an Olympus BX53F microscope (Tokyo, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were taken on a Nicolet 5700 IR spectrophotometer (Waltham, MA, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data for TBA-dots was measured by a VG Multilab 2000X instrument (Thermal Electron Corporation, USA). The circular dichroism (CD) spectra of the TBA and TBA-dots solution were collected with a Mos-450 spectrophotometer (Bio-logic, Grenoble, France) in PBS buffer (10mM, pH 7.4) at room temperature. The zeta potentials were measured on a Malvern Nano ZS90 (Worcs, UK).

The TBA-dots were prepared from 29-mer TBA by a hydrothermal process. TBA was dissolved in ultrapure water with the concentration of 7.0μM. Then, the TBA solution was transferred into a poly-(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 100°C for 12h. Finally, the uniform TBA-dots were obtained. The AuNPs were prepared by using citrate sodium as the reducing reagent (Grabar et al., 1995). The as-prepared AuNPs was characterized by an absorption maximum at 520nm and the concentration of AuNP solution was determinated to be 13nM by Beer's law; the absorption coefficient of 13nm AuNPs at 520nm is 2.7×108 M−1·cm−1 (Hill and Mirkin, 2006).

Detection of thrombin with multiple signals could be performed through the following two routes. Route A: 80nM TBA-dots were mixed with different concentrations of thrombin (0–160nM) and then incubated at 37°C for 1h in 10mM PBS buffer containing 50mM NaCl, 5mM KCl, 4mM MgCl2 (pH 7.4). One hour later, the mixture was added to AuNPs solution (70μL) and then their absorbance was measured by the UV-2450 spectrophotometer and their LS intensity and FL intensity was measured by the F-7000 spectrophotometer. Route B: 80nM TBA-dots and 176nM T2 were mixed with different concentrations of thrombin (0–40nM) and then incubated at 37°C for 1h. One hour later, the mixture was added to AuNPs solution (70μL) and their absorbance and LS intensity were measured. Absorption spectra were collected in a range from 400 to 800nm, FL spectra were measured from 350 to 570nm and light scattering spectra were obtained by scanning synchronously both the excitation and emission monochromators from 220 to 700nm with a slit width for excitation and emission of 5nm.

The typical TEM image of TBA-dots in Fig. 1a shows that TBA-dots are spherical in shape, uniform and monodisperse with an average size of 1.6±0.2nm. The corresponding AFM image reveals a typical topographic height of approximately 1.8nm (Fig. 1b). We further employed XPS to explore the composition and surface chemical states of TBA bio-dots. The full range XPS spectrum of TBA-dots (Fig. 1c) shows four peaks at 133.5, 284.6, 400.1, and 530.8eV, which are assigned to P2p, C1s, N1s and O1s, separately, revealing that the composition of the TBA-dots is close to that of TBA (Majumder et al., 2009). A high resolution O1s spectrum of the TBA-dots (Fig. 1d) displays four peaks at 530.8eV (PO4 1−), 532.0eV (O=C), 532.6eV (O–N) and 533.2eV (O–C), separately. It has been proved that DNA contains PO4 1− (Majumder et al., 2009), O=C and O–C, but DNA is absent of O-N (Guo et al., 2013). Therefore, it is highly possible that the O-N of TBA-dots are generated from the interaction between base pairs of TBA to form sp2 carbon–like centers as the luminescence centers or chromophores as previously reported (Li et al., 2015), resulting in the bright fluorescent TBA-dots. A high resolution N1s spectrum of the TBA-dots (Fig. S1c) exhibits three peaks at 398.4eV (–NH2), 400.0eV (–N=), and 401.4eV (–NH–), respectively (Majumder et al., 2009), suggesting that there are plenty of amine groups on the surface of TBA-dots. The functional groups of TBA-dots were further explored by FTIR characterization. As shown in Fig. 1e, the TBA-dots exhibit strong absorption band of N–H stretching vibrations at 3471cm−1 and two characteristic peaks of C–N stretching vibrations at 1418cm−1 and 1262cm−1 (Cheng et al., 2015), and the peaks at 1637cm−1 and 1165cm−1 indicate the existence of NH2 and C–NH–C groups (Song et al., 2015). These results are in accordance with XPS spectra, further confirming the existence of abundant amine groups on the surface of TBA-dots.

The optical properties of the as-prepared TBA-dots were investigated by UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra (Fig. 1f). The as-prepared TBA-dots exhibit two UV absorptions peaks, one is located at 260nm which is the characteristic absorption peak of DNA (Gallagher and Desjardins, 2008), while the other is in the range of 320–420nm which is a new peak of bio-dots (Guo et al., 2013). When excited from 310 to 350nm, the maximum emission is located at 425nm with a 320nm excitation and there is no remarkable shift of the maximum emission peak, indicating both the size and the surface state of the sp2 carbon-like centers in the TBA-dots should be uniform (Qu et al., 2013a; Zhuo et al., 2012). The TBA-dots aqueous solution shows yellowish under daylight and presents bright blue emission under the ultraviolet radiation (inset in Fig. 1f), representing the formation of TBA-dots with excellent fluorescence property. Furthermore, TBA-dots retain good flourescence performance even after storing for one month and irradiating under UV light for 1h (Fig. S2, curves a-c), demonstrates excellent stability of TBA-dots. As a contrast, TBA does not exhibit good fluorescent property (Fig. S2, curve d).

Since there are a large amount of amine groups on the surface of TBA-dots certified by XPS and FTIR (Fig. 1e, Fig. S1c), TBA-dots could assemble onto the surface of AuNPs through the Au-N coordination interactions (Deng et al., 2015). When reached to a certain concentration, the TBA-dots will be adsorbed onto the surface of AuNPs to form a shell, then induces AuNPs aggregation, which is similar to the phenomenon of CQDs which leads to the aggregation of AuNPs (Deng et al., 2015; Shi et al., 2014). As shown in Fig. S3a, when the concentration of TBA-dots increased, AuNPs showed decreasing absorbance at 520nm and increasing absorbance at 660nm which is attributed to the absorption band of the aggregated AuNPs, along with the color change from red to blue. Moreover, the absorbance ratio (A660/A520) increased from 0 to 80nM and then kept constant from 80 to 120nM (Fig. S3b). It is clearly illustrated that 80nM TBA-dots can totally make AuNPs aggregation in Fig. S3. Therefore, the optimal concentration of TBA-dots for constructing nanosensor is 80nM.

Plentiful TBA-dots (80nM) are adsorbed onto the surface of AuNPs to form a shell which induces AuNPs aggregation. Based on this phenomenon, a TBA-dots/AuNPs nanosensor is constructed. Thrombin includes two binding sites for the thrombin-binding aptamer (Li et al., 2012; Pavlov et al., 2004; Peng et al., 2013). Hence, it can act as bridges linking the TBA-dots/AuNPs together, with one thrombin molecule joining two TBA-dots/AuNPs (Peng et al., 2013). As results, two routes are designed for determination of thrombin with TBA-dots/AuNPs nanosensor based on the specific interaction between TBA-dots and thrombin. In route A, in the presence of thrombin, more AuNPs are aggregated together along with decrease in the absorbance peak of AuNPs at 520nm and red shift of the maximum absorption peak of AuNPs ( Fig. 2a). The maximum absorption peak shift of AuNPs (Δλmax) increases linearly with the increasing concentration of thrombin ranging from 8 to 160nM with a detection limit of 6.6nM (R2=0.997) (Fig. 2b). From another aspect, when more AuNPs are aggregated and the interparticle distance in the aggregates decreases, the larger size of scatters and interparticle plasmons coupling result in light scattering enhancement (Li et al., 2006). Therefore, the light scattering (LS) signals were collected for monitoring the concentration of thrombin. As expected, the LS peak intensity of AuNPs also increases with the increasing concentration of thrombin by route A and has a linear relationship in a range of 8–160nM with a detection limit of 7.5nM (R2=0.99) (Fig. 2c and d). Moreover, the maximum LS peak is located at 330nm instead of the absorption region at about 600nm, evidencing that the collected LS signals should be assigned to the conventional Rayleigh scattering instead of resonance light scattering (Pasternack et al., 1993). Furthermore, AuNPs are known to have an excellent quenching efficiency in abroad range of wavelengths. As a result, the FL of TBA-dots would be quenched by AuNPs when TBA-dots adsorb on the AuNPs since the emission spectrum of TBA-dots overlaps with the absorbance spectrum of AuNPs to some extent (Fig. S4, curves a and d), indicating the potential to produce efficient FRET (Deng et al., 2015; Shi et al., 2014). In route A, in the presence of thrombin, more AuNPs would be aggregated along with the decrease in peak intensity of AuNPs at 520nm and red shift of the maximum absorption peak for AuNPs, resulting in the reduced spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of TBA-dots and the absorbance spectrum of AuNPs (Fig. S4, curves a and e). Therefore, a decreased FRET is expected which results in fluorescence recovery of the TBA-dots (Fig. S4, curve b). As demonstrated in Fig. 2e, the quenched FL of TBA-dots recovered gradually with the increasing concentration of thrombin by route A. As shown in Fig. 2f, the FL intensity of TBA-dots increases linearly with the increasing concentration of thrombin ranging from 8 to 160nM with a detection limit of 7.4nM (R2=0.99).

In route B, hybridization of T2 with TBA results desorption of TBA-dots from the surface of AuNPs and thus AuNPs disperses in solution, since T2 is fully complementary to TBA. As shown in Fig. 3a, the absorbance peak of AuNPs at 660nm decreases sharply while the absorbance peak at 520nm increases in the presence of T2, accompanying with the color change from blue to red. Fig. 3b shows that the absorbance ratio (A660/A520) gradually decreases with the increasing concentration of T2 and levels off at higher concentration (>176nM). To achieve optimal detection performance, 176nM T2 is chosen for further experiments. In the presence of thrombin in route B, thrombin specifically interacts with TBA-dots instead of T2, resulting in more TBA-dots adsorbing onto the surface of AuNPs. Simultaneously it acts as bridges linking TBA-dots/AuNPs together, leading to AuNPs aggregation again. As shown in Fig. 3c and d, the absorbance ratio (A635/A523) increases linearly with the increasing concentration of thrombin from 0 to 35nM with a detection limit of 0.59nM (R2=0.99) by route B. Besides, the linear relationship between the increased LS intensity of AuNPs and the concentration of thrombin ranging from 0 to 40nM with a detection limit of 0.93nM (R2=0.99) by route B can be obtained in Fig. 3e and f. All the results show that the TBA-dots/AuNPs nanosensor with multiple signals could be successfully applied in sensitive determination of thrombin by two routes.

Nonspecific binding is a critical issue when using nanoparticles for sensing biomacromolecules. The specificity of this nanosensor for detection thrombin is determinated by challenging it with other proteins: bovine serum albumin (BSA), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), acetylcholin esterase (AchE), trypsin (Try), α-chymotrypsin (α-CT), hemoglobin (Hb). Non-target proteins and the target thrombin were incubated with the TBA-dots/AuNPs nanosensor for 1h by two routes, respectively.

As shown in Fig. S5, the significant changes of absorption spectra are only observed for the target thrombin and not for other non-target proteins by two routes, reflecting the excellent selectivity of the TBA-dots/AuNPs nanosensor for thrombin over other selected proteins.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of thrombin in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: Since that detection of thrombin in human blood or serum has the clinical significance, the practicality of this nanosensor has been further investigated. Herein, we investigate the relationship between A635/A523 and concentration of thrombin by route B as an example to demonstrate its practicality in spiked human serum samples. Several samples were prepared by spiking standard solutions of thrombin into human serum that has been diluted 100 fold by buffer to minimize nonspecific adsorption (Yue et al., 2014). Since that serum does not contain coagulation factors, the addition of thrombin does not affect the samples (Bini et al., 2007). As shown in Fig. S6, similar response for thrombin in serum to that in buffer (Fig. 3d) is observed. The linear range of 0–35nM with a detection limit of 1.05nM is obtained in spiked human serum samples. Besides, good stability of this nanosensor is also obtained in serum. The results demonstrate the promising practicality of this proposed nanosensor even in complex biological samples.

As mentioned above, TBA-dots adsorb on the surface of AuNPs forming a shell which induces AuNPs aggregation along with color change from red to blue. However, the dispersion state of AuNPs is not affect by adding equivalent pure TBA (Table S1). This clearly declares that TBA-dots induce the aggregation of AuNPs but not TBA. To understand the function of TBA-dots in the TBA-dots/AuNPs nanosensor, we measured the zeta potentials of the AuNPs, TBA-dots and AuNPs in the presence of TBA-dots, respectively ( Fig. 4a). The zeta potential of AuNPs was −30.1 mV due to citrate ions coating. The zeta potential of TBA-dots was −10.8 mV because of its negatively charged phosphate backbone. The zeta potential of AuNPs in the presence of TBA-dots was greatly reduced to −15.8 mV. In the presence of TBA-dots, a great amount of the weakly adsorbed citrate ions are replaced from the surface of the AuNPs by TBA-dots with low density of negative charge (Huang and Chang, 2007). TBA-dots adsorb onto the surface of AuNPs to form a “shell” which reduces the electrostatic repulsion between adjacent AuNPs, leading to the aggregation of AuNPs. As a control, the zeta potential of AuNPs in the presence of TBA slightly decreased from −30.1 mV to −28.3 mV, indicating that TBA has little effect on AuNPs under such circumstances.

Furthermore, the proposed sensing mechanism could be also revealed by circular dichroism (CD) spectra. As shown in Fig. 4b, both TBA-dots and TBA show a positive band at 265nm and a negative band at 235nm (black solid line, orange dashed line), which are the characteristic bands of the A-form duplex of hairpin structure (Bozza et al., 2006). However, TBA-dots show a more positive band and a more negative band than TBA at the same position, suggesting that TBA-dots retain the basic structure of TBA but form a more tightly folded structure via self-assembly which substantially increases the intensity of the positive and negative bands (Zheng et al., 2013). In the presence of thrombin, the intensities of the positive bands at 265nm of both TBA-dots and TBA rise obviously (Fig. 4b and Fig. S7, red solid line), indicating that more A-form duplex of hairpin structures are formed. Since that 29-mer TBA could interact with thrombin which is supported by the A-form stem formation (Lin et al., 2011), it clearly demonstrates that TBA-dots retain the quality of TBA to bind specifically with thrombin. In the presence of T2, the CD spectra of both TBA-dots and TBA display a negative band at 240nm and a positive band at 260–300nm which are the characteristic bands of A–T, and G–C base pairs (Fig. 4b and Fig. S7, green solid line) (Ratmeyer et al., 1994), indicating that both TBA-dots and TBA could hybridize with T2. As shown in Fig. 4b (blue solid line), the CD spectrum of TBA-dots in the presence of thrombin and T2 simultaneously is similar to the CD spectrum in the presence of thrombin, suggesting that TBA-dots have stronger binding capacity with thrombin than T2 which is similar to TBA (Fig. S7, blue solid line). In conclusion, the CD spectra demonstrate that TBA-dots retain the basic structure of TBA and could bind specifically with thrombin and hybridize with T2, moreover, TBA-dots have stronger binding capacity with thrombin than T2.

The mechanism is further confirmed by TEM images. Fig. 5a demonstrates the monodispersed AuNPs with an average diameter of about 13nm and the inset shows clear lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.236nm, corresponding to the lattice spacing of the (111) planes of Au (Kannan and John, 2008). In the presence of relatively high concentration of TBA-dots, AuNPs turn to aggregation (Fig. 5b) and the shell on the surface of AuNPs is clearly observed in Fig. 5c. After adding appropriate concentration of thrombin in route A, the aggregated TBA-dots/AuNPs nanosensor turn to aggregate to a greater extent (Fig. 5d). While in the presence of T2, as shown in Fig. 5e, the TBA-dots desorb from AuNPs and the aggregated AuNPs disperse again. However, after interacting with thrombin in route B, it is also obviously observed that TBA-dots adsorb onto the AuNPs to form a shell on the surface of AuNPs in Fig. 5f. These results are in accordance with the optical spectra, which clearly verified our proposed detection mechanism. The dark field microscopy (DFM) provides another convictive mean to image morphology changes of AuNPs. Under a dark field microscope, the size and brightness of scatters become larger and stronger because the aggregates are formed and then scatter more light (Nam et al., 2009). The dark field light-scattering images are also consistent with the optical spectra (Fig. S8), which further demonstrates the proposed mechanism.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized a new class of fluorescent bio-dots named TBA-dots via TBA self-assembly. After a simple, green, sustainable and low temperature of hydrothermal process, the obtained TBA-dots could emit a bright blue fluorescent light and induce the aggregation of AuNPs, while TBA could not. Moreover, TBA-dots retain the inherent structure of TBA to interact specifically with thrombin. Based on this, the thrombin-induced aggregation of TBA-dots/AuNPs nanosensor has been developed for the detection of thrombin with multiple signal readout by two routes. As far as we know, this is the first time to construct a bio-dots based nanosensor for detecting thrombin with multiple responses. The nanosensor needs no further surface functionalization of TBA-dots and AuNPs, and provides a sensitive method for the selective detection of thrombin. More importantly, this work may give a new sight for application of bio-dots in biosensing and preparation of aptamer-based nanomaterials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21105044, 21163014, and 21265017) and the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University (NCET-11-1002 and NCET-13-0848).
